questions.prompt: What is the defining difference between an entity and a value object?

options.text: Entities have identity and are compared by ID across state changes; value objects are entirely defined by their field contents.
Entities are stored in databases; value objects live only in memory.
Entities are classes; value objects must be records or structs.
Entities are large; value objects are small.

questions.explanation: Identity vs content is the whole distinction: two Customers with identical fields are different people; two `Money(5, USD)` are the same money. (b) values are persisted constantly (as embedded columns, JSON, etc.). (c) records make values *convenient*, but a hand-written final class is just as much a value object. (d) size is irrelevant — a polygon value object can hold thousands of points.

questions.prompt: A cache uses `Money` keys and users report entries disappearing. What is the mechanism?

options.text: Mutating a field used in hashCode after insertion leaves the entry in the old hash bucket, so lookups compute the new hash and search the wrong bucket.
HashMap requires keys to implement Comparable, which Money does not.
Objects.hash produces different values across JVM runs.
BigDecimal.equals compares scale, so 5.0 and 5.00 differ.

questions.explanation: The entry was filed under the hash of the *old* amount; after mutation, `get` hashes the *new* state and probes a different bucket — the entry still exists but is unreachable (and even same-bucket lookups fail the equals check against the mutated key... actually the stored key IS the mutated object; the lookup fails because the bucket is wrong). This is why hash keys must be immutable — make `Money` a record and the bug is unrepresentable. (b) HashMap needs hashCode/equals, not Comparable. (c) false within a run, which is what matters here. (d) is a real BigDecimal gotcha but would cause consistent misses from the start, not entries that vanish after a set-call.

questions.prompt: Why does replacing `transfer(String fromIban, String toIban, BigDecimal amount, String currency)` with `transfer(Iban from, Iban to, Money amount)` materially reduce defects?

options.text: Validation happens once at construction, the compiler rejects argument-order mistakes between the two IBANs and rejects passing raw strings, and currency logic gets a single home.
Fewer parameters always means fewer bugs.
Objects are passed by reference, which is faster than passing strings.
It prevents SQL injection in the persistence layer.

questions.explanation: Three concrete mechanisms: the constructor gate (invalid IBANs can't exist), type-checked call sites (swapping `amount` and `from` no longer compiles — though note from/to remain swappable with each other, which distinct types like `SourceAccount`/`TargetAccount` could fix), and one owner for money arithmetic instead of scattered `BigDecimal` handling. (b) parameter count barely changed. (c) performance is not the point and the claim is dubious. (d) injection protection lives elsewhere.

questions.prompt: Which benefits are genuinely caused by immutability (not merely correlated with good code)? Select all that apply.

options.text: Objects can be shared across threads without locks, since data races require a writer.
Invariants validated at construction hold for the object's entire lifetime.
Methods on immutable objects cannot throw exceptions.
Handing a reference to external code can never corrupt internal state.

questions.explanation: (a) no writes → no data races → no synchronization needed for safe publication (in Java, given `final` fields). (b) is immutability as encapsulation's endgame: one gate, checked once. (d) aliasing is only dangerous when someone can mutate through the alias. (c) is false — constructors and methods on immutable types throw freely (division by zero on Money, parse failures); immutability constrains *state*, not control flow.

questions.prompt: A service builds a 100k-character report by concatenating immutable Strings in a loop, and profiling shows GC pressure. What does the chapter's model recommend?

options.text: Confine mutation locally: build with a mutable StringBuilder inside the method, publish the final immutable String — mutation invisible outside one stack frame costs nothing in reasoning.
Switch the whole codebase to mutable string buffers for consistency.
Accept the GC cost — immutability must never be compromised.
Cache and reuse a shared static StringBuilder to avoid allocations.

questions.explanation: The builder pattern is immutability's designed escape hatch: locally scoped mutation, immutable result published. All reasoning benefits survive because no one else can observe the intermediate states. (b) globalizes a local optimization. (c) treats a default as a religion — the chapter explicitly prices this in. (d) introduces shared mutable state and a thread-safety bug to save an allocation: strictly worse.

questions.prompt: For a mutable `Order` entity holding line items, which API design follows the 'immutable at the boundary' guidance?

options.text: `void add(LineItem i)` for controlled mutation, plus `List<LineItem> items()` returning `List.copyOf(items)`
`List<LineItem> items()` returning the internal list, documented as 'do not modify'
Making Order a record so the whole entity is immutable, recreating it on every line change
`void setItems(List<LineItem> items)` so callers replace the list wholesale

questions.explanation: One writer (the entity itself, enforcing its invariants), snapshots out (callers can't corrupt state or observe torn updates). This is the chapter-1 + chapter-7 synthesis. (b) documentation is not enforcement — chapter 1's leak. (c) is defensible in some architectures but forfeits entity identity-with-lifecycle and forces every collaborator to thread new instances; the chapter's default keeps entities mutable behind gates. (d) hands invariant enforcement to every caller and stores a caller-owned mutable reference — two leaks in one.
